item.title: Burkina Faso, Mali and Niger Formally Begin Year-Long Withdrawal From the International Criminal Court
item.body:
Three West African nations have formally set in motion their departure from the International Criminal Court, opening a one-year countdown that could reshape the landscape of international justice. Niger deposited its written notification of withdrawal from the Rome Statute on June 18, 2026, with Burkina Faso and Mali following on June 24, according to confirmations from The Hague-based tribunal. Under the treaty's rules, the withdrawals will take effect one year after notification — in June 2027.

The move turns a political announcement into a legal reality. The three governments, all led by military authorities and joined together in the Alliance of Sahel States (AES), first declared their intention to leave the court in a joint statement in September 2025. The formal notifications to the United Nations Secretary-General now start the clock defined in Article 127 of the Rome Statute, the ICC's founding treaty.

## Why the Three Countries Say They Are Leaving

In their joint statement, the governments of Burkina Faso, Mali and Niger described the ICC as an instrument of what they called "neocolonial repression," arguing that the court has disproportionately focused its prosecutions on African countries while showing less appetite for cases involving more powerful states. Officials in the three capitals have said they intend to develop their own regional justice mechanisms, including a proposed Sahelian court that would handle serious crimes within the alliance's territory.

Similar criticisms have circulated among some African governments for more than a decade. Of the situations the ICC has formally investigated since it began operating in 2002, a majority have involved African countries — although several of those cases, including Mali's, were originally referred to the court by the African governments themselves. Mali asked the ICC to investigate crimes on its territory in 2012, a request that later led to two convictions related to the destruction of cultural and religious sites in Timbuktu.

## What the Withdrawal Does — and Does Not — Change

Legal experts note that leaving the Rome Statute is not an immediate exit, and it does not erase the court's existing authority. Key points include:

- **The one-year waiting period.** Each country remains a full state party, with all associated obligations, until its withdrawal takes effect in June 2027.
- **Past conduct stays within reach.** Article 127 specifies that withdrawal does not affect the court's jurisdiction over alleged crimes committed while a state was still a member. Investigations already underway, such as the ICC's long-running work on the situation in Mali, can legally continue.
- **Cooperation duties persist for pending matters.** A withdrawing state is not released from cooperation obligations connected to proceedings that began before its departure.

In practice, however, observers point out that the court depends heavily on state cooperation to gather evidence and execute arrest warrants, and that a government's decision to disengage can significantly complicate that work regardless of the formal legal position.

## Reaction From the Court and Rights Organizations

The ICC has publicly urged the three governments to reconsider, emphasizing the court's role as a venue of last resort for the most serious international crimes when national systems are unable or unwilling to act.

Human rights organizations have been sharply critical of the withdrawals. Human Rights Watch said the decision "betrays victims" of the region's armed conflicts, while Amnesty International warned that the departures risk entrenching impunity and could deny victims of serious abuses an avenue for justice and reparations. Parliamentarians for Global Action, a network of lawmakers, has also appealed to the three countries to remain within the Rome Statute system.

All three nations have faced years of armed conflict involving insurgent groups, and human rights monitors have documented allegations of serious abuses by multiple parties in the region. Supporters of the ICC argue that international oversight remains especially important in such contexts; the withdrawing governments counter that sovereignty and locally grounded institutions should take precedence.

## A Broader Pattern — and a Pivotal Moment

Burkina Faso, Mali and Niger would become only the third, fourth and fifth countries ever to complete a withdrawal from the ICC, after Burundi in 2017 and the Philippines in 2019. Other states, including South Africa and The Gambia, announced departures in the past but ultimately reversed course.

The timing carries added weight. The three Sahel states have already exited the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) and have deepened security and economic ties with partners outside the Western alliance system. Their coordinated departure from the ICC fits a wider strategy of replacing long-standing international frameworks with alliance-built alternatives — a test case for whether regional structures can credibly take on functions previously anchored in global institutions.

For the international justice system, the next twelve months will be closely watched. Whether the proposed Sahelian court materializes with genuine independence and capacity, and whether the ICC can preserve its ongoing cases in the region, will help determine if this moment marks a manageable setback or a deeper turning point for accountability worldwide.

*Sources: statements and reporting from the [International Criminal Court via Prism News](https://www.prismnews.com/news/burkina-faso-mali-and-niger-begin-withdrawal-from-icc), [Human Rights Watch](https://www.hrw.org/news/2026/07/02/sahel-countries-withdrawal-from-icc-betrays-victims), [Amnesty International](https://www.amnesty.org/en/latest/news/2026/07/burkina-mali-niger/), and [Parliamentarians for Global Action](https://www.pgaction.org/news/niger-burkina-faso-mali-withdrawals-icc.html).*
